coredata.dc:title: Surface enhanced FRET for sensitive and selective detection of doxycycline using organosilicon nanodots as donors
coredata.dc:creator: Huang, Shuyi
Yu, Long
Su, Pengchen
Wen, Tao
Sun, Mingtai
Huang, Dejian
Wang, Xiangke
Wang, Suhua
coredata.dc:description: In order to achieve on-site fluorescent detection of antibiotic doxycycline (DOC) residue, a new kind of fluorescent organosilicon nanodots (OSiNDs) was successfully constructed by a one-pot strategy and used as a novel ratiometric fluorescent probe. This method could enhance the fluorescence of DOC by conformational fixation on the surface of OSiNDs, and subsequently induced fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET). Control experimental results showed that phenylamine functional groups on the as-prepared OSiNDs possessed favorable interactions with DOC by hydrogen bond/π–π stacking, leading to both conformational change of DOC molecules and fluorescence resonance energy transfer process from OSiNDs to DOC. This method was then adapted and optimized for visual sensitive and selective detection of DOC based on ratiometric fluorescence signal, displaying a good linear range for DOC in the range of 1–35 μM with an excellent limit detection of ∼80 nM. Other tetracycline derivatives (TCs) and possible interferences have little influence on the determination of DOC using the method. The spike and recovery test of DOC in real water samples demonstrated the feasibility of the OSiNDs for DOC assay in practical samples and a test paper was fabricated with OSiNDs loading, achieving easy and visual detection of DOC by naked eyes.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Organosilicon nanodots
Doxycycline
Surface enhanced fluorescence
Antibiotics detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence responses to other antibiotics and metal ions
Study of spectral properties and fluorescence stability of OSiNDs
Ratiometric fluorescence assay of OSiNDs towards DOC
Mechanism study for fluorescence detection of OSiNDs towards DOC
OSiNDs with different concentrations of DOC
Selectivity and interference of OSiNDs based ratiometric fluorescence method toward DOC
Visual detection of DOC by test paper
Recovery test of doxycycline in real river water and ground water

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 21775042 , 22176044 , and 82001957 ), the National Key Research and Development Program of China ( 2017YFA0207003 ), and Projects of Talents Recruitment of GDUPT ( 2019rc056 , and 2019rc057 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) UV–Vis absorption spectra and (B) Emission spectra of probe OSiNDs, DOC, and the mixture of DOC and OSiNDs in PBS buffer solution (pH = 6.5), [DOC] = 35 μM.
Schematic illustration for mechanism of ratiometric detection to DOC by OSiNDs.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of OSiNDs in the presence of different concentrations of DOC. All spectra were measured under the same experimental conditions (λex = 365 nm). (Inset: photographs of the OSiNDs dispersion taken under UV lamp with λ = 365 nm before (left) and after (right) the addition of 35 μM of DOC). (B) Fitting curves between relative fluorescence decrease and the concentrations of DOC. (PBS buffer: 10 mM, pH 6.5).
Structure of tetracyclines (TCs). Chlortetracycline (CTC), doxycycline (DOC), tetracycline (TC), minocycline (MOC), and oxytetracycline (OTC) have similar chemical structures.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of OSiNDs in the presence of DOC, TC, DOC, CTC and MOC ([TCs] = 30 μM) under optimal conditions, respectively, and (B) corresponding quantized fluorescence responses (F517/F448). Statistical significance was calculated via one way analysis of variance (ANOVA) with a Tukey posthoc test: ∗p < 0.05, ∗∗p < 0.01; (C) Anti-interference ability of OSiNDs for the detection of DOC.

Photos of the test papers loaded with or without OSiNDs under (A) daylight lamp and (B) 365 nm UV lamp illumination. (C) Photos of the test papers after the addition of 10 μL of DOC solution with different concentrations (from left to right: 0, 5, 15, 25, 35 μM).
Schematic illustration of fabrication of OSiNDs and detection of DOC based on the ratiometric fluorescence platform.
Recovery test of doxycycline spiked in real river water and ground water.

head.ce:title: Surface enhanced FRET for sensitive and selective detection of doxycycline using organosilicon nanodots as donors

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. College of Environmental Science and Engineering, North China Electric Power University, Beijing, 102206, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Environmental Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In order to achieve on-site fluorescent detection of antibiotic doxycycline (DOC) residue, a new kind of fluorescent organosilicon nanodots (OSiNDs) was successfully constructed by a one-pot strategy and used as a novel ratiometric fluorescent probe. This method could enhance the fluorescence of DOC by conformational fixation on the surface of OSiNDs, and subsequently induced fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET). Control experimental results showed that phenylamine functional groups on the as-prepared OSiNDs possessed favorable interactions with DOC by hydrogen bond/π–π stacking, leading to both conformational change of DOC molecules and fluorescence resonance energy transfer process from OSiNDs to DOC. This method was then adapted and optimized for visual sensitive and selective detection of DOC based on ratiometric fluorescence signal, displaying a good linear range for DOC in the range of 1–35 μM with an excellent limit detection of ∼80 nM. Other tetracycline derivatives (TCs) and possible interferences have little influence on the determination of DOC using the method. The spike and recovery test of DOC in real water samples demonstrated the feasibility of the OSiNDs for DOC assay in practical samples and a test paper was fabricated with OSiNDs loading, achieving easy and visual detection of DOC by naked eyes.

ce:section.ce:para: Tetracyclines (TCs), a class of antibiotics with a broad antibacterial spectrum activity and bacteriostatic activity, have been extensively used in the livestock husbandry and the treatment of certain bacterial infections such as pneumonia, bronchitis and urinary tract infections [1–3]. However, the use of sub-therapeutic doses of antibiotics conduced the undesirable release of residues into nature, which caused the drug-resistant microorganisms to spread and imposed substantial risk to individuals with hypersensitivity to tetracyclines [4–6]. To avoid food safety problems, there is an imperative for the evaluation of antibiotic residue in foods and domestic water. Doxycycline (DOC), as a kind of tetracycline derivatives, is of particularity attention due to its most common use in food-producing animals approved by the European Union and the United States Food and Drug Administration [7]. Hence, an agile approach with high sensitivity and selectivity needs to be proposed for the detection and quantification of doxycycline in aqueous solution.

At present, there are many analytical approaches applying to the detection of antibiotic residues, including immunoassay [8,9], high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) and microbial detection [10] and so on. However, most of these approaches are time-consuming and costly, often require sophisticated instrument and complicated extraction procedures. Meanwhile, the poor reproducibility also poses negative impact on large-scale sample analysis, providing immense challenges for implementation of fast and convenient detection.

Fluorescent assay is considered as a superior approach owing to its wider application scenarios [11,12]. In recent years, various photoluminescent probes, including metal nanoclusters [13,14], nanoparticles [15–17] and organic fluorescent probes [18], have been developed for detection application, but most of them have their own insuperable shortcomings. Metal nanoclusters are generally unstable and prone to aggregation, while organic fluorescent probes, such as photoluminescent olefinic and aromatic materials, suffer from expatiatory synthetic procedure and rigorous reaction conditions, which require costly vacuum evaporation instruments for its implementation [19,20]. In these cases, nanoparticles, especially metal-free nanoparticles, aroused great interest among researchers due to their safety and biocompatibility, which avoid the potentially risk from heavy metals.

Silicon-containing nanodots (SiNDs), an emerging and attractive class of photoluminescent metal-free nanoparticles, have gained a considerable attention due to their excellent characteristics including favorable optical properties, facile surface functionalization, typically photostability and cost-effective silicon sources [21–23]. It has been widely applied in various areas such as biosensing and imaging [8,20,24], theranostic materials [25], organic solar cells [21] and drug delivery [23]. Recently, great effort has been focused on the fluorescence properties of SiNDs toward antibiotics. For example, Wang et al. [26] synthesized a kind of single emission probe (SiO2-AF@MIPs) towards TC based on quenching mechanism. However the weak fluorescence in the presence of high analytes concentration limits their practical application. Li et al. [22] fabricated a blue emission SiNPs functionalized by Eu3+ to construct ratiometric fluorescent probe. It showed obvious color change that could be identified by naked eyes, but the post-synthesis modification complicated its operation process. Xu et al. [27] synthesized luminescent silicon-based nanoparticles as a ratiometric platform for the detection of oxytetracycline, its practical application was still limited by the relatively harsh reaction conditions. Herein, we designed a facile and mild strategy to synthesize a new kind of organosilicone nanodots (OSiNDs) using one-step hydrothermal reaction between 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES) and 4-(Diphenylamino) phenylboronic acid (DLPA), which enable uniform functionalization on the surface of OSiNDs with phenylamine functional groups. The blue emitting OSiNDs show a high photoluminescence quantum yield (QY) of ≈33%. In the presence of DOC, the blue fluorescence could be quenched while new green emitting peaks appeared, which enable OSiNDs to act as a ratiometric fluorescent platform for the detection of DOC without any post-synthesis modification by expensive metallic elements. The mechanism of the ratiometric fluorescent detection can be concluded in two simultaneous processes (Scheme ): (1) The blue emission can be quenched by DOC owing to the inner filter effect (IFE) and fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET); (2) The amino group on the surface of OSiNDs formed a hydrogen bond with DOC, while benzene ring interacted with DOC by π–π stacking. Under these two intermolecular forces, the OSiNDs and DOC were in close proximity, resulting in molecule conformational change of DOC from weak-fluorescent twisted type to strong-fluorescent extended type. Furthermore, the FRET process from OSiNDs to DOC further enhanced the green emission with the increasing concentration of DOC. Based on the above phenomenon, the OSiNDs achieves ratiometric fluorescent determination in 100% aqueous medium and displays excellent sensitivity and selectivity toward DOC. In addition, the visual detection of DOC with OSiNDs was carried out in test paper, which offers the possibility of qualitative determination and semi-quantitative recognition towards DOC on-site.

All chemicals were of analytical grade and without further purification. 3-Aminopropyltriethoxysilane (98%) (APTES), 4-(Diphenylamino) phenylboronic acid (DLPA) were purchased from Aladdin Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd (China). Norfloxacin (NOC), chloramphenicol (CAP), penicillin (PEN), ceftriaxone (CRO), streptomycin (SM), erythromycin (ERM), glutathione (GSH), bovine serum albumin (BSA), ascorbic acid (Vc), histidine (His), glucose (Glu), lysine (Lys) and all kinds of metal chlorides were obtained from the China National Medicine Corporation and used as received. All the solutions were prepared with water purified by a Millipore water purification system (18.2 MΩ cm).

0.01 g DLPA and 1 mL APTES was dissolved in 4 mL of water, the mixture was transferred into a 10 mL Teflon-lined autoclave, which was sealed and maintained at a temperature of 160 °C for 2 h. After cooling down to the room temperature, the resultant solution was dialyzed (500 Da) for 24 h in room temperature and condense by rotary evaporator. The final sample was collected through freeze drying and stored at 4 °C for use. The yield of OSiNDs was calculated to be 28.2% based on APTES.

For investigation of the morphology of OSiNDs, transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was acquired on FEI Tecnai G2 F20 electron microscope at 200 kV. The Fourier transformed infrared (FTIR) spectra were obtained on the Nicolet Magana-IR 750 spectrometer using the KBr method. The ultraviolet spectrum analysis of B-OSiNDs was conducted with a TU-1901 spectrometer (PERSEE, Beijing, China). The thermogravimetric (TG) measurement was performed using a TG-50 thermal analyzer under air at a heating rate of 15 °C min−1 in nitrogen atmosphere. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectrum analysis of OSiNDs was performed using a Shimadzu XRD-600 diffractometer (Kyoto, Japan). Fluorescence experiments were recorded by a PerkinElmer LS-55 luminescence spectrometer. The X-ray photon spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were collected on the ESCALAB 250 Xi XPS of Thermo Scientific to achieve the detailed information.

The QY of OSiNDs was tested by single-point measurement using quinine sulfate (QY = 0.54 in 0.1 M H2SO4, at 360 nm) as standard:
(1)
Q Y
( % )
= Q
Y q
I s
A q
(
η s
)
2
I q
A s
(
η q
)
2
where I was the fluorescent emission intensity, A is the absorbance, and η is the refractive index of the solvent. The subscripts q and s signify fluorescent standard samples and experimental samples, respectively.

In order to ensure the accuracy and repeatability of the experiment, some preparations should be made before the fluorescence experiment. DOC was directly dissolved in ultrapure water to get the stock solution at the concentration of 1.0 mM. The stock solution was prepared freshly and used at room temperature. The fluorescence response upon the interaction between DOC and OSiNDs was carried out in the optimal conditions (1ⅹ10 PBS buffer, pH = 6.5). To measure DOC concentration in PBS buffer solution, 200 μL of stock solution (5 mg/mL) of OSiNDs in Milli-Q water was first diluted in 1.8 mL of PBS 6.5 buffer to obtain a final probe solution with concentration of 0.5 mg/mL in a quartz cell (1 cm path length). For the sensitivity experiment of the as-obtained OSiNDs to DOC, different volumes of DOC were added to the diluted probe solution to obtain final reaction system with the DOC concentrations of 0, 1, 3, 5, 8, 12, 16, 20, 25, 30 and 35 μM, respectively. After reaction for 1 min, the fluorescence spectra were observed in the range from 385 to 700 nm using a 365 nm excitation wavelength with a 400 nm/min scan rate. The quantitative analysis of two fluorescence signals (448 nm and 517 nm) was carried out by comparing the intensity of the maximum emission peak with the blank sample (only OSiNDs and PBS with pH 6.5).

To investigate the mechanism of DOC detection by OSiNDs, two componential groups (Aniline and Aniline@SiO2) were prepared and then recorded their emission spectra with or without the addition of DOC (35 μM). Aniline group was prepared by dissolving Aniline in PBS buffered water (pH = 6.5, CAn = 0.2 mg/mL). Aniline@SiO2 was prepared by mixing silicone powder (0.5 mg/mL) and Aniline (0.2 mg/mL) in PBS buffered water (pH = 6.5, CAn = 0.2 mg/mL) and stirred for 2 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence responses to other antibiotics and metal ions
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, the specific recognition of DOC among the tetracycline derivatives (TCs) or other common antibiotics was studied by adding stock solutions of analytes into OSiNDs diluted solution and comparing their fluorescence signals with DOC group. The final concentrations of norfloxacin (NOC), chloramphenicol (CAP), penicillin (PEN), ceftriaxone (CRO), streptomycin (SM) and erythromycin (EM) and other metal ions were 30 μM, respectively. For anti-interference study, several kinds of antibiotics and metal ions were selected as coexisting substances. The concentration of additional interferences was 10 times more than that of DOC. In closing, the DOC was added into the above detection system and FL signals were subsequently recorded.

The test paper was fabricated by loading OSiNDs on polypropylene membrane. In detail, the round papers were firstly obtained by hole punch with a diameter of 8 mm and then immersed into OSiNDs (1 mg/mL) solution for 5 min. The naturally dried test papers were used for the detection of DOC by the naked eyes.

The morphology of the as-obtained OSiNDs was firstly investigated by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). As shown in Fig. S1A, the TEM image clearly revealed that the OSiNDs displayed an ultrasmall spherical shape but most part of them slightly aggregated together due to the electrostatic interaction between surface functional groups. After statistical analysis by ImageJ, the primary size of these obtained OSiNDs was estimated to be around 4.2 nm (Fig. S1B). Then crystalline structure of OSiNDs was assessed by X-ray diffraction (XRD) (Fig. S1C). The sharp peak at 2θ = 23° and 45° was assigned to carbon composite and board peaks at 36.5°, 39.5° and 60° found in the latter are consistent with the standard data of Si–C (JCPDS No. 22–1301). In general, the as-synthesized samples comprised of C and Si–C in an amorphous compound.

In order to further explore the functional groups and chemical composition in OSiNDs, Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy, Thermogravimetric (TG) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) experiments were employed for more detail analysis. For the FTIR spectra (Fig. S1D), three peaks were observed in APTES at the range of 2800 cm−1–3000 cm−1, which was confirmed to be related to the stretching vibration of C–H, verifying the simultaneous coexistence of CH3 and CH2. After hydrothermal treatment with DLPA to obtain OSiNDs, the peaks at 2960 cm−1 disappeared, which was ascribed to the hydrolyzation of methoxy group and finally demonstrated that only CH2 exists in OSiNDs. Moreover, the peak associated with stretching vibration of Si–O (∼1090 cm−1) disappeared and two new peaks appeared in FTIR spectrum of OSiNDs, which were assigned to the stretching vibrations of Si–OH (∼840 cm−1) and Si–O–Si (∼1140 cm−1) respectively. This phenomenon demonstrated the construction of a silica-like structure via the partial cross-linking between different silicon alcohol molecules during hydrothermal process [28]. From the XPS spectrum (Fig. S2A), five apparent peaks (e.g., O1s, C s, N1s and Si2s and Si2p) can be observed from the XPS spectrum, suggesting the main elements in OSiNDs sample were C, O, N and Si. In the high resolution XPS spectrum of Si2p (Fig. S2B), the signal was assigned to three peaks (Si–C, 101.8 eV; Si–O–H, 102.4 eV; Si–O–Si, 103.1 eV) and the O1s spectrum contained the Si–O signal at 532.4 eV, which was in accordance with the previous conclusion that OSiNDs were formed by the hydrolyzation of silicon-oxygen bond and condensation reaction between Si–OH. Moreover, the high resolution XPS spectra of N 1s, C 1s and O1s revealed the presence of various oxygen or nitrogen containing functional groups (e.g., –OH, –COOH and NH2) on the surface of OSiNDs (Figs. S2C–E). Particularly, the atomic ratio of these four elements (Si, C, N, O) was estimated to be 1:3.5:1.7:1. Compared with silicon source (APTES), the losing ratios of carbon and oxygen in OSiNDs supported the hydrolysis and etherification reaction during hydrothermal treatment. Similar conclusion could be demonstrated by showing the typical TG−derivative thermogravimetry and DTA thermographs of the sample (Fig. S2F). The first mass loss (∼9.8 wt%) below 150 °C and the endothermic peak at 105.5 °C was on account of the removal of free water and structural water present in the material [29]. The mass loss ranged from 200 °C to 380 °C (∼5 wt%) accompanied by an exothermic peak at 295.5 °C was ascribed to oxidation of amino group, while the mass loss between 380 °C and 600 °C (∼22 wt%) related to the burning of carbon branched chain. The loss mass above 600 °C was corresponding to the fracture of C–O–Si and the formation of Si–O–Si.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study of spectral properties and fluorescence stability of OSiNDs
ce:section.ce:para: The optical characteristics of OSiNDs were performed through UV–vis and fluorescence spectra. As displayed in Fig. S3A, the absorption band of the OSiNDs was located at the range of 250 nm–400 nm and the absorbance gradually increased with the decrease of wavelength, which is consistent with characteristic of the indirect band gap [8]. Accordingly, the fluorescence emission spectrum of OSiNDs were taken out under different excitation wavelengths below 400 cm−1. One can see that the position of emission wavelength occurred red shift with the increasing of excitation wavelength from 320 nm to 380 nm. Such excitation-dependent FL behavior could be ascribed to the difference in particle sizes and surface states of the OSiNDs [30]. Also, the optimum emission and excitation wavelengths are located at 448 and 365 nm, respectively, in consideration of emission wavelength and maximum fluorescence intensity (Fig. S3B). In addition, the quantum yield of OSiNDs was measured carefully to be 33% under the optimal excitation wavelength, which is a very competitive value compared with most fluorescent probes (Fig. S4). In order to evaluate the photostability of OSiNDs, its fluorescence properties were investigated under different conditions including UV irradiation time, pH value of the solution, and storage time (Figs. S5–7). The fluorescence intensity of OSiNDs maintained the same level under continuous illumination with a UV lamp for 1 h and storing at room temperature for 30 days. Moreover, the fluorescence intensity is almost unchangeable at a pH range of 3–9, implying the negligible effect of pH value to the fluorescence property of OSiNDs. Given these points, OSiNDs was convinced to exhibit excellent fluorescence performance and stability that could be considered as a potential fluorescence sensor for the detection of DOC in aqueous solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ratiometric fluorescence assay of OSiNDs towards DOC
ce:section.ce:para: The determination of degradation residue in aqueous solution is one of the potential application scenarios for fluorescent detection. In the degradation process of tetracycline antibiotics, the elimination efficiency is deeply affected by various conditions such as temperature and pH value. Therefore, appropriate optimization of detection conditions is necessary to obtain better sensor performance. Initially, The UV–vis absorption spectra of bare DOC, OSiNDs and the mixture of DOC and OSiNDs were displayed in Fig. 1 A. After the coordination with OSiNDs, a remarkable red shift from 354 nm to 377 nm was observed for the characteristic absorption peak of DOC, accompanied by the increase of absorbance value. The results indicate that DOC was coordinated with OSiNDs, which eventually resulted in the structural change of DOC. The fluorescence pre-response of OSiNDs toward DOC was investigated in PBS 6.5 solution without incubation time (Fig. 1B). After the addition of moderate DOC solution, the emission peak of OSiNDs at 448 nm was partly quenched while a new fluorescence peak at 517 nm was observed. But most particularly, this significant fluorescence peak cannot be observed from the emission spectrum of bare DOC, indicating that the strong fluorescence at 517 nm is attributed to the interaction between OSiNDs and DOC. Such changes in spectra fluorescence and UV–vis spectra suggested that OSiNDs might be allowed as a ratiometric fluorescent probe for DOC detection. Subsequently, the pH effect on the fluorescence of the OSiNDs/DOC compounds with or without a certain period of incubation time were investigated (Fig. S8). We used F517/F448 as a signal for the detection of DOC and four different pH values were chosen (i.e. 5.5 6.5, 7.5 and 8.5) in this study. Clearly, the F517/F448 signals in weakly acid environment (pH = 5.5 or 6.5) did not change substantially with increasing incubation time from 0 to 10 min, suggesting the immediate and stable interaction between OSiNDs and DOC. This interaction might be attributed to the electrostatic interaction between the amino group of DOC and carboxyl group on the surface of OSiNDs. While in the weak alkaline environment (pH = 7.5 or 8.5), the signals of F517/F448 were higher than in acidic environment, which might be ascribed to the conformation for most tetracyclines in alkaline solution. The amino group on DOC was deprotonated in alkaline environment and more likely to form hydrogen bond to induce conformational change. Notably, this hydrogen bond is unstable then lead to the decreasing of the signals of F517/F448 with the incubation time increasing. Therefore, the determination of DOC by OSiNDs should be carried out in acidic condition without incubation time, in spite of its relative weaker signal than that in alkaline solution. Furthermore, one can see that the signal of F517/F448 is almost not influenced with the increasing of temperature (Fig. S9). Given these points, PBS buffer at pH 6.5 was appropriate as the assay conditions for ratiometric fluorescence determination of DOC without temperature control and incubation time.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism study for fluorescence detection of OSiNDs towards DOC
ce:section.ce:para: Considering aforementioned spectroscopic phenomenon, it is confirmed that OSiNDs can serve as a potential ratiometric fluorescent probe for the detection of DOC. To examine the mechanism for fluorescence quenching of OSiNDs and increasing green fluorescence by DOC, the UV–vis absorption, fluorescence spectra and fluorescence life measurement were studied subsequently. As displayed in Fig. S10, it was observed that DOC band ranging from 200 to 400 nm possesses a broad absorption whereas the OSiNDs has excitation peak centered at 365 nm (λem = 448 nm), indicating that the quenching of OSiNDs at 448 nm possibly take place via internal filtration effect (IFE) process. Besides, the fluorescence lifetime of OSiNDs is 3.53 ns. While with the addition of DOC at the concentration of 15 and 30 μM, the fluorescence lifetime decayed to 1.94 ns and 1.11 ns, respectively (Fig. S11). The decreasing fluorescence lifetime clearly verified the occurrence of energy transfer between OSiNDs and DOC, indicating the quenching of OSiNDs was not only affected by IFE process because the fluorescence lifetime is generally invariable for IFE. The absorption and emission properties of the mixture of OSiNDs and DOC were investigated due to immediately construction of OSiNDs/DOC complex have been verified in the previous part (Fig. S12). After binding with DOC, obvious spectral overlap was also observed between the absorption spectrum of OSiNDs/DOC complex and emission of OSiNDs, suggesting the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET)-based steady quenching mechanism [31].

In order to study the reason behind the occurrence of new green emission of OSiNDs toward DOC, the emission and excitation spectra of DOC were first investigated in both weakly acid and alkaline solution (Fig. S13). One can see that bare DOC in PBS buffered solution showed the emission band at around 530 nm, which was very close to the position of OSiNDs/DOC complex. Similar to the OSiNDs/DOC complex, the fluorescence intensity of DOC at pH = 7.5 was higher than that at pH = 6.5(Fig. S8). These phenomenon indicated that the green emission of OSiNDs/DOC complex might be ascribed to the self-fluorescence of DOC, which was caused by the conformational instability of DOC in different solution [32,33]. To further investigate the occurrence of enhancing green fluorescence of OSiNDs/DOC complex, the emission spectra of five groups (OSiNDs + DOC, Aniline + DOC, Aniline@SiO2 and Aniline@SiO2 + DOC, bare DOC) were carried out (Fig. S14). The FL intensity of Aniline + DOC was higher than that of bare DOC, indicating the aniline groups can assist self-fluorescence enhancement of DOC. After aniline groups gathered on the surface of SiO2, the green emission was further enhanced due to the stronger π–π stacking interaction between Aniline@SiO2 and DOC. Most importantly, the OSiNDs + DOC group showed strongest fluorescence at 517 nm compared with other groups, which was ascribed to the richer and stronger aniline groups on the surface of OSiNDs. The abundant aniline groups would interaction with DOC by hydrogen bonding and π–π stacking and induced a conformational change of DOC from weak-fluorescent twisted type to strong-fluorescent extended type. In spite of that, the FL intensity of Aniline@SiO2 + DOC was still much lower than that of OSiNDs + DOC group, indicating that conformational change of DOC was not the main reason for the higher fluorescence of OSiNDs/DOC complex at 517 nm. In this case, FRET process from OSiNDs to DOC occurred due to the close distance between each other by π–π stacking and hydrogen bond, giving the opportunity to construct ratiometric fluorescent-based senser for DOC which can be applied to actual sample detection (Fig. 2 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: OSiNDs with different concentrations of DOC
ce:section.ce:para: Under the optimized conditions, the sensing experiments were carried out to measure the ability of OSiNDs in detecting trace amounts of DOC. The fluorescence response of OSiNDs (λex = 365 nm) upon addition of different concentrations of DOC in an aqueous system were recorded (Fig. 3 A). The fluorescence emission at 488 nm from OSiNDs gradually weakened whereas the emission peak at 517 nm progressively increased with increasing concentrations of DOC. Fig. 3B shows the relationship between the concentration of DOC and the fluorescence intensity ratio (F517/F448). The signal of F517/F448 is linearly increased with the increases of DOC concentration from 0 to 35 μM (R2 = 0.982) with a linear equation expressed as: (F517/F448) = 0.029 [DOC] +0.290. The limit of detection (LOD) was estimated as 80 nM based on the ratio of 3σ/slope (σ is the relative standard deviation of blank sample), which is lower than the maximum residue limit of DOC in drinking water (0.225 μM) ruled by the European Union [15,34]. The linear range and detection limit of OSiNDs were compared with other sensors and shown in Table S1 [30,35–37]. More importantly, the fluorescence of OSiNDs showed a remarkable color evolution toward DOC, which can be observed by naked eye under a UV lamp (insert picture in Fig. 3A). The OSiNDs exhibits lower LOD and ratiometric detection without any supplemental additive towards DOC, which is superior to most silicon-based quantum dots reported before.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and interference of OSiNDs based ratiometric fluorescence method toward DOC
ce:section.ce:para: The selective experiments towards other kinds of antibiotics (NOC, CAP, PEN, CRO, SM, ERM) and some potential interfering species (K+, Na+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Co2+, Fe3+, Al3+, GSH, BSA, Vc, His, Glu and Lys) were carried out and illustrated in Fig. S16. Obviously, no significant change was observed from the fluorescence signal (F517/F448) for these potential interferences, confirming the OSiNDs has high selectivity toward DOC. Moreover, the anti-interference experiment of OSiNDs for DOC was performed by adding nearly 10-fold of interfering species (Fig. 5C). It can be seen that the existence of these interfering species caused negligible changes compared with that of bare DOC, proving the high selectivity of OSiNDs towards DOC compared with other biological reagents or metal ions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Visual detection of DOC by test paper
ce:section.ce:para: The test papers based on OSiNDs were developed to detect DOC. Under daylight lamp, no obvious color change was observed with the loading of OSiNDs (Fig. 6 A). However, the test paper loaded with OSiNDs shows strong blue fluorescence under the 365 nm UV light illumination (Fig. 6B). Upon addition of different concentrations of DOC, the fluorescence of prepared test paper immediately changed from blue to green in varying brightness levels (Fig. 6C). Such fluorescence color changes on the test paper roughly means the concentration of DOC in aqueous solution, revealing the practical application of this transducer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Recovery test of doxycycline in real river water and ground water
ce:section.ce:para: The practical application of OSiNDs was evaluated by determination of recovery of spiked doxycycline in real river water and ground water. The river water samples were obtained from the Pingshui River (Jiangxi, China), and ground water samples from the well of Wugong Mountain (Jiangxi, China). The river water and ground water were simply filtered through 0.45 mm Supor filters to remove particulate suspension. The recovery study was carried out on the mixture of PBS buffered water (pH = 6.5) and water samples (2:1, v/v) spiked with 0, 10, 20 and 35 μM doxycycline. The analytical results are shown in Table 1 . The contents of doxycycline in both river water and ground water without spiked doxycycline are below the LOD of the method (80 nM), so the values are not considered in the calculation of recoveries. Noted that the results obtained in real water samples showed good recovery values, which confirmed that the proposed sensor was applicable for practical doxycycline detection in real samples with other potentially competing species coexisting.

In this work, we have synthesized a new kind of organosilicon nanodots (OSiNDs) by a facile strategy and applied it for the determination of DOC with high sensitivity and selectivity. The detection mechanism was based on two parts including the quenching of emission peak from OSiNDs and a fluorescence enhancing of DOC itself by FRET under the influence of OSiNDs, which enable OSiNDs act as a dual mode fluorescent probe for the determination of DOC with a blue-to-green color evolution. The quantitation of DOC by the ratio fluorescence intensity of F517/F448 exhibits a good linear relationship with a detection limit of 80 nM, which is superior to many other probes that have been reported. The recovery test of DOC in real water samples demonstrated the feasibility of the OSiNDs probe for DOC monitoring in practical samples. This work not only provide a promising approach for rapid on-site detection of DOC, but also offers new insight for constructing efficient ratiometric or turn-on fluorescence platform.

Shuyi Huang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Software, Writing – original draft. Long Yu: Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Pengchen Su: Writing – review & editing. Tao Wen: Writing – review & editing. Mingtai Sun: Supervision, Writing – review & editing, Investigation. Dejian Huang: Writing – review & editing. Xiangke Wang: Writing – review & editing. Suhua Wang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21775042, 22176044, and 82001957), the National Key Research and Development Program of China (2017YFA0207003), and Projects of Talents Recruitment of GDUPT (2019rc056, and 2019rc057).

ce:section.ce:para:
